response: Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-abc", "next_review_date": "2026-04-15"}]. The next_review_date parameter directly sets when the project appears for review, overriding the calculated schedule.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-xyz", "completed_by_children": true}]. This enables auto-completion when the last child task is completed.

Call get_tasks with task_id for the recurring task (or query='Weekly team standup'). The returned fields include nextDueDate, which shows the next occurrence's due date without needing to complete the current one. No tool call needed beyond a get_tasks lookup.

Call get_projects with include_task_health=true. This returns availableCount per project. Filter the results for projects where availableCount is 0 (and status is active). These are stalled projects with no available actions. Alternatively, use stalled_only=true to get projects OmniFocus considers stalled.

No tool call needed for the explanation. The behavior depends on the catchUpAutomatically field. Call get_tasks with the task ID to check this field. If catchUpAutomatically is true, completing it creates one catch-up occurrence (not 5). If false, each missed interval spawns its own occurrence, so you could get multiple. The repeatSummary and catchUpAutomatically fields from get_tasks tell you what will happen.

No tool call strictly needed — this is an interpretation question. Check get_tags to see if the 'Priority' parent tag has childrenAreMutuallyExclusive: true. If it does, adding 'Low' via update_tasks add_tags=['Low'] will silently remove 'High' (the sibling). This is expected OmniFocus behavior with mutually exclusive tag groups. The user should be warned that 'High' will be removed automatically.

Call create_projects with [{"name": "Q3 Report", "folder_path": "Work", "due_date": "2026-07-15", "defer_date": "2026-06-01"}]. The defer_date hides the project until June 1, and due_date sets the July 15 deadline.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-abc", "due_date": ""}]. Passing an empty string clears the due date.
Call get_projects with project_id='proj-xyz'. The response includes dueDate, deferDate, and plannedDate fields.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-101", "parent_task_id": "task-200"}]. This moves the task from the inbox to become a subtask of 'Draft report'. Note: project_id and parent_task_id are mutually exclusive on create, but update_tasks accepts parent_task_id to reparent.
Call get_tasks with query='mortgage'. This searches task names and notes case-insensitively across all projects.

Call get_tags (no parameters). The response includes parentTagId for each tag. Find the tag named 'High' and check its parentTagId. Then find the tag whose ID matches that parentTagId to confirm it's named 'Energy'.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-500", "status": "dropped"}]. This marks the task as dropped (abandoned) without deleting it. Note: this task is not recurring, so no need to clear recurrence first.

Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-A", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-B", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-C", "status": "dropped"}]. This batch-drops all three projects without deleting them.

No tool call needed — this is an interpretation question. Per the server instructions: tasks in a completed project show completed: false but available: false. The task's own completed field reflects only the task's state, not the container's. Use get_tasks with available_only=true to exclude these non-actionable tasks. They appear in unfiltered results because their own completed flag is false.
Call reorder_project with project_id='proj-C', before_project_id='proj-A'. This places proj-C before proj-A, making it first in the folder.

Two calls in sequence: 1) create_tags with [{"name": "Priority", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}]. 2) create_tags with [{"name": "High", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Medium", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Low", "parent_tag": "Priority"}]. The parent tag's childrenAreMutuallyExclusive ensures assigning one child removes siblings.

Call update_tags with [{"id": "tag-energy-001", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}]. This enables mutual exclusivity on the existing parent tag, so from now on assigning one child energy level will remove siblings.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-website-001", "flagged": true, "add_tags": ["High Priority"]}]. This flags the project and adds the tag in a single call.
Call reorder_task with task_id='task-qa-001', before_task_id='task-fe-001'. This moves QA testing before Frontend build.
Call delete_tags with tag_ids='tag-errands-001'. This permanently deletes the tag. Tasks that had this tag lose the association but are not deleted.
Call get_perspectives (no parameters). Filter the results for entries where type is 'custom' to see only custom perspectives.
Call create_folders with [{"name": "Q2", "parent_path": "Work"}]. This creates a Q2 folder nested inside the Work folder.
Call switch_perspective with perspective_name='Weekly Review'.

Two steps: 1) Call get_tasks with overdue=true to see all overdue tasks. 2) After reviewing results and identifying the 3 most urgent, call update_tasks with [{"id": "<id1>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id2>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id3>", "flagged": true}] to flag them.

First call get_projects with query='Quantum Computing Research' to check if the project exists and get its ID. If it exists, call get_tasks with project_id=<the_project_id>. If get_projects returns no results, inform the user that no such project exists.
Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Buy birthday gift", "tags": ["Computer", "Errands"]}]. No project_id means it goes to the inbox. Tags are passed as a list of strings.

Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Buy groceries"}, {"task_name": "Do laundry"}, {"task_name": "Wash dishes"}, {"task_name": "Vacuum living room"}, {"task_name": "Prep meals for the week"}]. No project_id places them in the inbox.
Call create_projects with [{"name": "Kitchen Renovation", "project_type": "sequential", "folder_path": "Home"}, {"name": "Garden Planning", "project_type": "parallel", "folder_path": "Home"}].

First create the parent task as a sequential action group: call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Event Planning", "project_id": "proj-100", "sequential": true}]. Then, using the returned task ID, call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research venues", "parent_task_id": "<event_planning_id>"}, {"task_name": "Book venue", "parent_task_id": "<event_planning_id>"}, {"task_name": "Send invitations", "parent_task_id": "<event_planning_id>"}]. The sequential=true on the parent ensures subtasks must be done in order.

Step 1: Call create_projects with [{"name": "Home Renovation", "project_type": "parallel"}]. Step 2: Using the project ID, create two sequential action groups: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Contractor Quotes Chain", "project_id": "<proj_id>", "sequential": true}, {"task_name": "Paint Selection Chain", "project_id": "<proj_id>", "sequential": true}]. Step 3: Create subtasks under each group: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Get contractor quotes", "parent_task_id": "<quotes_chain_id>"}, {"task_name": "Schedule contractor", "parent_task_id": "<quotes_chain_id>"}] and [{"task_name": "Choose paint colors", "parent_task_id": "<paint_chain_id>"}, {"task_name": "Buy paint", "parent_task_id": "<paint_chain_id>"}]. The parallel project allows both chains to proceed simultaneously, while sequential action groups enforce order within each chain.

Step 1: Create the parent task: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "project_id": "proj-200", "sequential": true}]. Step 2: Create sequential subtasks using the parent ID: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research topic", "parent_task_id": "<prepare_id>"}, {"task_name": "Write outline", "parent_task_id": "<prepare_id>"}, {"task_name": "Create slides", "parent_task_id": "<prepare_id>", "sequential": false}]. Note: 'Create slides' is set to sequential=false (parallel) since its subtasks can happen in any order. Step 3: Create parallel subtasks under 'Create slides': create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Design template", "parent_task_id": "<slides_id>"}, {"task_name": "Add content", "parent_task_id": "<slides_id>"}, {"task_name": "Add animations", "parent_task_id": "<slides_id>"}].

OmniFocus cannot model true diamond dependencies (task depends on two independent predecessors). The closest approximation: create a sequential action group. Step 1: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Diamond Group", "project_id": "proj-300", "sequential": true}]. Step 2: Under it, create a parallel sub-group for the independent tasks, then the dependent task: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Independent Work", "parent_task_id": "<group_id>", "sequential": false}, {"task_name": "Review findings", "parent_task_id": "<group_id>"}]. Step 3: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Draft report", "parent_task_id": "<independent_id>"}, {"task_name": "Collect data", "parent_task_id": "<independent_id>"}]. The outer sequential group ensures 'Review findings' only becomes available after the parallel 'Independent Work' action group completes (all its subtasks done). This correctly models the diamond dependency.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-weekly-123", "recurrence": "", "status": "dropped"}]. Per the server instructions, you MUST clear recurrence first (recurrence='') before dropping, otherwise dropping a recurring task spawns the next occurrence. Sending both fields in a single update handles this correctly.

Call update_tags with [{"id": "tag-aimee-001", "parent_tag": "People"}]. The parent_tag parameter accepts a tag name (not ID) and moves the tag under the specified parent. Existing task associations are preserved — only the tag's position in the hierarchy changes.
